# First Chapter {#chapterOne}

This is an R Markdown document. Markdown is a simple formatting syntax for authoring HTML, PDF, and MS Word documents. For more details on using R Markdown see <http://rmarkdown.rstudio.com>.

When you click the **Knit** button a document will be generated that includes both content as well as the output of any embedded R code chunks within the document. You can embed an R code chunk like this:

```{r cars}
summary(cars)
```


## Including Plots

You can also embed plots, for example:

```{r pressure, echo=FALSE, fig.cap="Pressure", fig.align="center", fig.asp = 0.7, out.width="50%"}
plot(pressure)
plot(pressure)
```

You can also add a reference to Figure \@ref(fig:pressure)

Note that the `echo = FALSE` parameter was added to the code chunk to prevent printing of the R code that generated the plot.


##With some references and equations

Display a reference [@Adams1965] and a numbered equation:

\begin{equation}
  
  \begin{split}
  
      \quad S = & \beta_S + \beta_{SR} R + \beta_{SGG} GG + \beta_{SRGG} R \times GG + \epsilon_{S} \\
      
                & \beta_S + \beta_{SR} R + \beta_{SGG} GG + \beta_{SRGG} R \times GG
  
  \end{split}

  (\#eq:eqtest)
  
\end{equation}


and add a reference to this eqauation \@ref(eq:eqtest)

To add an image while controlling the display options:

```{r, echo=FALSE, fig.align="center", fig.asp = 1, out.width="33%"}
knitr::include_graphics(rep("images/logoRUG.gif", 1))
```


##Tables

```{r table}

knitr::kable(
  list(
    head(iris[, 1:2], 3),
    head(mtcars[, 1:3], 5)
  ),
  caption = 'A Tale of Two Tables.', booktabs = TRUE
)

```


and to reference it: \@ref(tab:table)
